sections.title: What belongs here
sections.summary: Social worlds, creator platforms, persistent shared games, and any product where user content, permissions, moderation, and publishing workflows are core to the platform.

sections.title: Core structural rule
sections.summary: Separate creation, moderation, publication, and live runtime. Treat permissions and ownership as architecture, not policy text.

sections.title: Preferred project structure
sections.summary: - creator-facing content and tooling - permission and ownership model - publishing pipeline - live runtime and persistent data - moderation and safety systems - rollback and recove

sections.title: Source-of-truth boundaries
sections.summary: Canonical sources should usually define: - creator roles and permissions - publishing targets and version identities - moderation policies and enforcement hooks - world or experien

sections.title: Verification model
sections.summary: Verification needs to cover social and operational risk, not only code behavior. Temper should encourage: - permission-boundary tests - publish and rollback rehearsal - moderation-

sections.title: Release and hotfix concerns
sections.summary: The publishing surface is itself a product feature. Release mistakes can expose private content, break creator trust, or corrupt persistent worlds. Before shipping: - verify permis

sections.title: Failure modes
sections.summary: - permission models implied in code instead of made explicit - creators blocked by unclear ownership or publish rules - persistent state changes with no migration safety - moderati

sections.title: Team operating notes

sections.title: Best Temper overlays
sections.summary: - `Kaplan 🎯` for player and creator trust - `Carmack 🔧` for permissions, rollback, and operational rigor - `Wright 🌱` for creative leverage and social possibility space
